artifact.description: Peer-reviewed manuscript: Bayesian hierarchical model for predicting carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae transmission in hospital settings

ai_assistance.description: Feature analysis on genomic sequencing data, partial correlation computation for confound investigation, and comparison of model features against published resistance mechanisms
Suggested model architectures and hyperparameter ranges based on dataset characteristics; assisted with sensitivity analysis code generation and literature review drafting

review.notes: PI reviewed full manuscript and methodology; requested expanded discussion of confound exclusion decision in methods section
Journal peer review — accepted with minor revisions; praised confound analysis, requested additional sensitivity analysis on prior selection
Journal peer review — accepted with minor revisions; requested clarification on temporal cross-validation split rationale

decisions.description: Excluded ICU bed count feature from the final predictive model after stratified analysis revealed it was a confound for antibiotic prescribing intensity and patient acuity
decisions.context: AI feature analysis ranked icu_bed_count as the 3rd strongest predictor of CRE transmission (AUC improvement of 0.03). However, larger hospitals have more ICU beds, more complex patients, and higher antibiotic use — the feature may capture institutional complexity rather than a causal mechanism. Including a confounded feature risks misleading infection control teams at smaller hospitals and producing a model that fails to generalize.

aiInputs.contribution: Computed partial correlations controlling for antibiotic DDDs and patient acuity score; icu_bed_count predictive rank dropped from 3 to 47 after adjustment

decisions.rationale: Partial correlation analysis confirmed the feature is confounded. Clinical validation by Dr. Nakamura corroborated: community hospitals with fewer ICU beds but high antibiotic use show comparable CRE rates. A model built on confounded features may appear accurate in training but will mislead clinical decision-making. Scientific integrity requires excluding features without plausible causal mechanisms, even at the cost of a modest AUC reduction.

decisions.description: Selected Bayesian hierarchical model with hospital-level random effects over gradient-boosted trees and temporal convolutional network alternatives
decisions.context: Dataset has multi-hospital structure (14 sites), class imbalance (rare transmission events), and temporal dependencies. Model must produce uncertainty estimates for clinical decision-making — point predictions are insufficient when the consequence is resource allocation affecting patient safety.

aiInputs.contribution: Compared three architecture options (gradient-boosted trees, temporal CNN, Bayesian hierarchical model) with trade-off analysis on interpretability, uncertainty quantification, and multi-level structure handling

decisions.rationale: Bayesian hierarchical model naturally handles multi-hospital structure via random effects, quantifies uncertainty through posterior distributions (critical for clinical use), and produces interpretable results. Gradient-boosted trees would require post-hoc uncertainty calibration. Temporal CNN offers no interpretability advantage and requires more data than available. AI-suggested priors were reviewed against three meta-analyses and two were adjusted based on a 2025 study not in the AI's training data.

decisions.description: Classified two AI-identified novel predictive features as hypotheses for future wet-lab validation rather than established mechanisms
decisions.context: AI comparison of model's top 10 features against curated CRE resistance mechanism database found 8 aligned with published literature and 2 with no prior published evidence. The temptation is to claim discovery; the risk is overstating computational findings without experimental confirmation.

aiInputs.contribution: Compared model's top predictive features against curated database of known CRE resistance mechanisms; flagged 2 features with no matching published mechanism

decisions.rationale: Computational identification of a statistical association is not evidence of a biological mechanism. Claiming novel resistance mechanisms without wet-lab confirmation would overstate the findings and could misdirect future research. The features are reported in the discussion as hypothesis-generating, with explicit language distinguishing computational prediction from mechanistic validation.

entities.label: Published manuscript: CRE transmission prediction model
Curated genomic-epidemiological dataset (2,847 isolates, 14 hospitals, restricted access)
Raw whole-genome sequencing data from 14 hospital microbiology labs
Patient ward transfer histories and antibiotic prescribing records
Partial correlation analysis results for confound investigation

activities.description: Lena Bergmann assembled and cleaned genomic-epidemiological dataset from 14 hospital sources with documented preprocessing pipeline

AI performed unsupervised analysis of genomic features — clustering resistance gene cassettes, identifying co-occurring mobile genetic elements, ranking 340 candidate features by correlation with transmission events

interaction.responseNotes: Accepted genomic and behavioral feature rankings as starting points for investigation. Flagged structural features (especially icu_bed_count) for confound testing rather than accepting AI ranking at face value.

activities.description: Nkechi designed stratified analysis to test whether icu_bed_count was confounded; AI computed partial correlations; result confirmed confound; feature excluded from model
AI computed partial correlations controlling for antibiotic DDDs and patient acuity score across hospital size tertiles

interaction.responseNotes: Statistical computation accepted as correct. Interpretation — that icu_bed_count is confounded — was made by Nkechi based on the results combined with clinical input from Dr. Nakamura.

activities.description: AI suggested three model architectures with trade-off analysis; Nkechi selected Bayesian hierarchical model and modified AI-suggested priors

interaction.responseNotes: Selected Bayesian hierarchical model from three options. Accepted suggested prior structure but adjusted 2 of 5 prior distributions based on a 2025 meta-analysis not in AI training data.

activities.description: Nkechi trained model using temporal cross-validation; ran sensitivity analyses on prior selection and feature exclusion; AI assisted with sensitivity analysis code generation
AI generated sensitivity analysis pipeline code

interaction.responseNotes: Accepted pipeline structure; added additional diagnostic checks for posterior convergence and added prior predictive checks not included in AI output.

activities.description: Dr. Nakamura validated model predictions against ward-level CRE outbreak observations in the test period; confirmed top 3 predicted high-risk wards matched actual outbreak wards
AI compared model's top 10 predictive features against curated CRE resistance mechanism database

interaction.responseNotes: Accepted alignment confirmation for 8 of 10 features. For 2 novel features flagged by AI as having no published mechanism, Nkechi classified them as hypothesis-generating rather than claiming discovery.

activities.description: Nkechi wrote the manuscript; AI assisted with literature review drafting and figure descriptions
AI generated initial draft of literature review section

interaction.responseNotes: Substantially rewrote for voice, accuracy, and inclusion of recent publications. Verified all AI-cited references exist and are correctly characterized.

activities.description: Dr. Mensah reviewed full manuscript and methodology; requested expanded confound discussion in methods section
Two anonymous peer reviewers evaluated the manuscript; both accepted with minor revisions
Nkechi addressed reviewer comments, added additional sensitivity analysis, and submitted final revision; manuscript accepted for publication

relationships.notes: Nkechi directed the analysis and evaluated outputs against domain knowledge
Nkechi designed the stratified analysis and interpreted results
Dr. Nakamura provided clinical corroboration from ward-level observations
Paper describes and reports results of the model

policyEvaluations.notes: Protocol approved for retrospective analysis of de-identified genomic and epidemiological data. AI-assisted analysis covered under approved protocol amendment (Oct 2025). Patient-level data restricted to authorized research team with signed data use agreements.

AI involvement disclosed in methods section per journal requirements: AI used for feature analysis, statistical computation, model architecture comparison, literature review drafting, and code generation. All AI outputs reviewed and modified by human researchers. AI is not listed as an author.

Code deposited in public repository with version tags. Model deposited in model registry with training configuration. Dataset deposited in restricted-access repository with data dictionary and preprocessing pipeline. Temporal cross-validation splits documented. Random seeds recorded. Prior distributions and sensitivity analysis results reported.

Human authorship and accountability confirmed at every stage. AI assistance fully disclosed. All AI outputs reviewed by domain experts. Decisions documented with rationale. No AI output reached final artifacts without human scientific judgment.
